title: D'Arcy Oil Concession
description: William Knox D'Arcy granted a 60-year concession to explore for oil across most of Persia, the origin of the country's oil economy.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica

title: First major oil strike at Masjed Soleyman
description: Discovery of commercially viable oil deposits in Khuzestan, leading to the founding of the Anglo-Persian Oil Company in 1909.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Pahlavi dynasty founded
description: Reza Khan crowned Reza Shah Pahlavi, beginning a state-led modernization and industrialization program.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Bank Melli Iran established
description: Iran's first national bank founded, taking over currency-issuing functions previously held by the British Imperial Bank of Persia.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica

title: Trans-Iranian Railway completed
description: State-financed railway from the Persian Gulf to the Caspian Sea, funded largely through domestic sugar and tea consumption taxes rather than foreign loans.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica

title: Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran
description: Britain and the USSR jointly invade to secure oil supply lines and the Persian Corridor; Reza Shah abdicates in favor of his son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Plan Organization established; First Seven-Year Plan
description: Sazman-e Barnameh created to design and coordinate national development plans, beginning the era of formal five/seven-year planning.
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University)

title: Oil industry nationalized
description: Majlis votes to nationalize the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company under Prime Minister Mohammad Mossadegh; National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC) subsequently established.
source_name: CIA National Intelligence Survey 33: Iran — The Economy

title: 1953 coup d'état
description: CIA- and MI6-backed coup removes Prime Minister Mossadegh and restores the Shah's executive authority, ending the oil nationalization standoff.
source_name: Foreign Relations of the United States (State Dept. Office of the Historian)

title: Consortium Agreement
description: A consortium of Western oil majors resumes Iranian oil operations under a profit-sharing agreement, ending the nationalization dispute.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica

title: White Revolution launched
description: Shah's referendum approves a reform package including land redistribution, forestry nationalization, and profit-sharing for industrial workers.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Oil price shock
description: OPEC price increases following the Arab oil embargo roughly quadruple Iran's oil revenue, fueling a large-scale but overheated state spending boom (Fifth Development Plan, 1973-78).
source_name: OPEC Annual Statistical Bulletin

title: Anti-profiteering campaign
description: Government price-control crackdown on merchants amid inflation from oil-boom overheating, contributing to bazaari discontent ahead of the revolution.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Economy ix. In the Pahlavi Period)

title: Islamic Revolution
description: Mohammad Reza Shah's government falls; the Islamic Republic is proclaimed under Ayatollah Khomeini on 1 April 1979.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Nationalization of banks and major industries
description: Revolutionary Council nationalizes the banking system and large private industrial/commercial conglomerates, restructuring much of the economy under state and bonyad control.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Economy x. Under the Islamic Republic)

title: US freezes Iranian assets
description: Executive Order 12170 freezes roughly $12 billion in Iranian assets in the US following the embassy hostage crisis (Nov 4, 1979 – Jan 20, 1981).
source_name: USIP Iran Primer — Timeline of U.S. Sanctions

title: Iran-Iraq War begins
description: Eight-year war (1980-1988) imposes massive fiscal costs, disrupts oil exports, and entrenches a rationing/coupon system for basic goods.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: US trade embargo
description: United States severs diplomatic relations and imposes a full trade embargo on Iran.
source_name: USIP Iran Primer — Timeline of U.S. Sanctions

title: First Post-War Five-Year Plan (Rafsanjani reconstruction)
description: Rafsanjani government begins post-war economic liberalization and reconstruction planning after Khomeini's death (June 1989).
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University)

title: Exchange-rate unification attempt
description: Rafsanjani government attempts to unify Iran's multiple-tier exchange rate system; the effort partially unwinds after reserve pressure.
source_name: IMF Iran country page (Article IV history)

title: US comprehensive trade and investment ban
description: Executive Order 12959 bars virtually all US trade and investment with Iran.
source_name: OFAC — Iran Sanctions Program Information

title: Iran-Libya Sanctions Act (ILSA)
description: US law imposes secondary sanctions on foreign firms investing over $40 million/year (later $20M) in Iran's energy sector.
source_name: USIP Iran Primer — Timeline of U.S. Sanctions

title: Official/market exchange-rate unification
description: Iran unifies its official and market exchange rates at the start of the 1381 fiscal year, a rare successful unification episode.
source_name: IMF Iran country page (Article IV history)

title: First UN Security Council sanctions
description: UNSC Resolution 1737 imposes the first multilateral sanctions over Iran's nuclear program, targeting proliferation-sensitive trade and assets.
source_name: Wikipedia — International sanctions against Iran (cross-check chronology skeleton)

title: UNSC Resolution 1929
description: Expanded UN sanctions targeting Iran's financial sector, arms trade, and Revolutionary Guard-linked entities.
source_name: USIP Iran Primer — Timeline of U.S. Sanctions

title: Targeted Subsidies Reform Law implemented
description: Energy prices raised 3-9x and bread prices doubled; ~90% of households enrolled in a monthly cash-transfer program (~$45/person), one of the largest unconditional cash-transfer schemes in the world, costing ~10% of GDP in 2010.
source_name: IMF Working Paper — Iran: The Chronicles of the Subsidy Reform

title: SWIFT disconnection and oil-export sanctions
description: Major Iranian banks cut off from SWIFT messaging; US NDAA sanctions target foreign purchasers of Iranian oil, triggering a sharp rial depreciation through 2012-13.
source_name: USIP Iran Primer — Timeline of U.S. Sanctions

title: JCPOA signed
description: Iran and the P5+1 sign the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, exchanging nuclear program limits for sanctions relief.
source_name: Arms Control Association — JCPOA at a Glance

title: JCPOA Implementation Day
description: IAEA certifies Iranian compliance; US, EU and UN lift nuclear-related sanctions on oil, banking, shipping and other sectors, unlocking roughly $56bn of previously frozen assets.
source_name: OFAC — JCPOA Implementation Day actions

title: US withdraws from JCPOA
description: President Trump announces US withdrawal from the JCPOA and reimposition of sanctions after 90/180-day wind-down periods (effective Aug 7 and Nov 5, 2018).
source_name: Wikipedia — US withdrawal from the JCPOA (cross-check against OFAC primary orders)

title: First snapback sanctions take effect
description: Executive Order 13846 reimposes sanctions on Iran's automotive sector, precious-metals trade, and rial-denominated transactions.
source_name: OFAC — May 2018 Guidance on Reimposing Sanctions

title: Second snapback: oil and banking sanctions
description: Remaining JCPOA-lifted sanctions on oil exports, shipping, and the Central Bank of Iran come back into full effect, driving a sharp rial crash through late 2018.
source_name: OFAC — Sanctions reimposition guidance

title: Gasoline price hike triggers nationwide protests
description: Government raises subsidized fuel prices and imposes rationing overnight; protests spread to 190+ cities ('Bloody Aban'); Amnesty International verified 304 deaths in the state crackdown; a 4-day nationwide internet shutdown followed.
source_name: NPR

title: FATF blacklists Iran
description: Financial Action Task Force places Iran on its blacklist over anti-money-laundering/counter-terror-financing standards, adding friction to remaining international banking channels.
source_name: Financial Action Task Force

title: Preferential "4,200 toman" import-currency rate eliminated
description: Parliament approves ending the subsidized foreign-currency allocation for essential imports (flour, medicine), removing the ~$10-14bn/yr subsidy and roughly doubling bread prices for many bakeries.
source_name: Bourse & Bazaar Foundation

title: Mahsa Amini protests
description: Death of Mahsa Amini in morality-police custody triggers months of nationwide unrest, disrupting business activity and accelerating rial depreciation.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: President Raisi dies in helicopter crash
description: President Ebrahim Raisi's death triggers a snap presidential election; markets and the rial reacted to the political uncertainty.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Pezeshkian elected president
description: Reformist-aligned Masoud Pezeshkian wins the presidential runoff on a platform including negotiating sanctions relief.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Sugar and tea consumption tax enacted
description: Majlis approves indirect consumption taxes on sugar and tea, the principal domestic revenue source used to finance construction of the state-built Trans-Iranian Railway without foreign loans.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Fiscal System v. Pahlavi Period)

title: Capitulations abolished
description: Iran unilaterally ends the 19th-century capitulations regime under which foreign nationals held extraterritorial legal immunity from Iranian courts, asserting full Iranian judicial sovereignty over foreign nationals.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Commerce vii. In the Pahlavi and post-Pahlavi periods)

title: Tariff autonomy law enacted
description: New customs tariff law (11 Ordibehesht 1307) restores Iran's autonomy to set its own import tariffs, cancelling Qajar-era treaty limits on Iranian customs duties and introducing an ad valorem tariff structure.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Commerce vii. In the Pahlavi and post-Pahlavi periods)

title: Progressive income tax introduced
description: Iran's first progressive income tax law sets marginal rates from 0.5% to a maximum of 4%, though it applies mainly to public-sector wage and salary earners and yields limited government revenue.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Fiscal System v. Pahlavi Period)

title: Foreign Trade Monopoly Law
description: Majlis grants the government a monopoly over all foreign trade, empowering it to set import quotas tied to non-oil export proceeds and to establish state trading companies for sugar, tea, opium, tobacco and cereals -- the legal basis for an import-substitution industrialization strategy.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Economy ix. In the Pahlavi Period)

title: D'Arcy oil concession unilaterally cancelled
description: Reza Shah's government cancels the 1901 D'Arcy oil concession over royalty disputes, prompting Britain to refer the standoff to the League of Nations and the Permanent Court of International Justice; the dispute is resolved by a new 60-year concession agreed in 1933.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: D'Arcy oil concession renegotiated
description: New 60-year concession with the Anglo-Persian Oil Company, ratified by the Majlis on 28 May and given royal assent the next day, reduces the concession area by three-quarters and guarantees Iran a fixed per-ton royalty plus 20% of the company's distributed profits.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: State-led industrialization drive accelerates
description: Backed by high protective tariffs and Bank Melli financing, state and private investment establish roughly 265 new plants (textiles, sugar refining, cement, matches, glass) between 1930 and 1940, with industrial growth peaking in 1934-38 under direct state direction.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Industrialization i. The Reza Shah Period and Its Aftermath 1925-53)

title: Second Seven-Year Plan launched
description: Plan Organization launches Iran's second national development plan (1955-1962), financed substantially by oil revenue following the 1954 Consortium Agreement; communications, agriculture and the Khuzestan Development Service (Karaj, Sefid Rud and Dez dams) receive the largest budget allocations.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Barnama-Rizi / Planning)

title: First national census of Iran
description: The General Department of Public Statistics conducts Iran's first modern population and housing census (1-16 November 1956) using 27,607 enumerators, recording a population of 18,954,704.
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University) -- Census

title: Central Bank of Iran established
description: The Monetary and Banking Act of 7 Khordad 1339 (28 May 1960) creates Bank Markazi-e Iran, transferring central-banking functions -- currency issue, bank supervision, monetary policy -- from Bank Melli Iran to a dedicated central bank with initial capital of 3.6 billion rials.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Bank-e Markazi-e Iran)

title: First IMF stand-by arrangement and stabilization program
description: Facing a balance-of-payments crisis after falling oil prices leave reserves covering barely one to two weeks of imports, Iran draws on a 35m-SDR IMF stand-by arrangement and adopts an austerity program that sharply curbs bank credit expansion and government spending through 1962.
source_name: Iranian Studies (Cambridge University Press) -- Balance of Payment Deficit and Inflation in Iran 1955-1962

title: Land Reform Law first stage enacted
description: Prime Minister Ali Amini's government, acting through Agriculture Minister Hassan Arsanjani, enacts a Land Reform Law limiting individual landownership to one village and mandating sale of excess land to tenant farmers at controlled prices -- the opening measure later folded into the White Revolution program.
source_name: The Cambridge History of Iran - Land Reform in Iran

title: Literacy Corps founded
description: Government decrees of 26 October and 3 December 1962 establish the Sepah-e Danesh (Literacy Corps), conscripting educated young men into military-alternative service teaching literacy in villages; parliament approves the program on 26 January 1963 as part of the White Revolution.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Literacy Corps)

title: Industrial profit-sharing law enacted
description: As one of the six White Revolution measures approved by national referendum, factory workers and employees at large private enterprises are granted a 20% share of net profits; cumulative profit-sharing payouts rise 128-fold between 1963 and 1975.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica (White Revolution)

title: Forests and rangelands nationalized
description: The White Revolution referendum approves nationalization of all forests and pastureland; the measure is later formalized as the Nationalization of Forests Act, ratified by parliament and implemented in 1967, ending private and tribal ownership of Iran's woodlands.
source_name: PMC (National Library of Medicine) -- Impact of Iran's Forest Nationalization Law on Forest Cover Changes

title: Second national census
description: The Statistical Centre of Iran conducts the country's second population and housing census (30 October - 19 November 1966).
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University) -- Census

title: General Income Tax Law reform
description: Iran overhauls its income tax code, introducing a unified progressive schedule with marginal rates spanning 10% to 55%, though income tax remains a small share of total government revenue relative to oil.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Fiscal System v. Pahlavi Period)

title: Tehran Agreement raises oil prices
description: Iran and five other Gulf OPEC producers sign the Tehran Agreement with Western oil majors, raising the tax rate on net income from 50% to 55% and posted prices by 21% (38 cents/barrel), with scheduled annual increases through 1975; Iran's per-barrel oil revenue is projected to rise from 87 cents to $1.37 by FY1975.
source_name: Foreign Relations of the United States (State Dept. Office of the Historian)

title: Persepolis 2500th anniversary celebrations
description: Shah hosts a multi-day celebration of 2500 years of Persian monarchy at Persepolis for dozens of visiting heads of state and royalty. Official Iranian figures put the cost at $16.6-16.8m, while contemporaneous Time magazine estimates ran as high as $100m; the event became a symbol of fiscal excess ahead of the revolution.
source_name: Wikipedia (cross-checked cost figures per Time magazine and official Iranian statements)

title: Sale and Purchase Agreement replaces Consortium
description: A 20-year Sale and Purchase Agreement (effective retroactively from 21 March 1973) ends the 1954 Consortium Agreement's operating structure: NIOC assumes full ownership, operation and control of Iran's oil industry, with former consortium members continuing service and offtake through the new Oil Service Company of Iran (OSCO).
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica (Oil Agreements in Iran)

title: Great Civilization spending surge -- Fifth Plan revised
description: Following the 1973-74 oil price quadrupling, government oil revenue rises from $5bn to $19bn in a year; the Shah revises the Fifth Development Plan, nearly doubling total planned investment from $36.5bn to $70bn and raising overall government spending from $44bn to $123bn, fueling severe absorptive-capacity bottlenecks and inflation.
source_name: Intereconomics (Naini) -- The Fifth Development Plan

title: Rastakhiz single-party system established
description: Shah dissolves Iran's existing two-party system and creates the Rastakhiz (Resurgence) Party as the country's sole legal political party, declaring that citizens who reject it are free to leave the country; the party becomes the organizing vehicle for the 1975-77 anti-profiteering campaign.
source_name: Middle Eastern Studies (Taylor & Francis) -- A Single Party State in Iran 1975-78: The Rastakhiz Party

title: Anti-profiteering campaign launched
description: Shah decrees price-control enforcement the White Revolution's 14th principle; Rastakhiz Party task forces raid bazaar shops nationwide, and by August 1976 roughly 25,000 alleged violators have been jailed or fined, deeply alienating the bazaari merchant class ahead of the revolution.
source_name: Iranian Studies (Cambridge University Press) -- State Repression in the Iranian Bazaar 1975-1977

title: Credit squeeze collapses construction boom
description: A sharp rise in inflation combined with a Central Bank credit squeeze ends the oil-boom-fueled construction and building boom that had drawn international contractors to Iran since the early 1970s, sharply slowing investment and GDP growth.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica (Iran -- The White Revolution)

title: Fourth national census
description: The Statistical Centre of Iran conducts the 1976 population and housing census (30 October - 18 November 1976), enumerating roughly 33.7 million people using nearly 30,000 census workers.
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University) -- Census

title: Amouzegar austerity program
description: Shah appoints technocrat Jamshid Amouzegar prime minister with a mandate to curb inflation through fiscal austerity; his government cuts public spending, halts development projects and tightens credit, deepening unemployment and business distress as revolutionary unrest builds.
source_name: The Washington Post (8 August 1977)

title: Oil workers' strike paralyzes economy
description: Nationalized oil-refinery workers strike beginning in late October 1978, demanding political prisoners' release; by November a general strike shuts refineries, banks, ports and factories, cutting oil output from roughly 6 million to 1.5 million barrels/day and costing the government an estimated $60m per day.
source_name: Brookings Institution -- What Iran's 1979 Revolution Meant for US and Global Oil Markets

title: Wartime rationing coupon system introduced
description: Amid the Iraqi invasion, a trade squeeze and food shortages, the government begins distributing ration coupons for sugar, rice, meat, cooking oil and other staples through neighborhood cooperatives; the coupon ('koopon') system remains in force through the 1980s and is phased out only toward the end of the Ahmadinejad era.
source_name: IMF Working Paper -- Iran: The Chronicles of the Subsidy Reform

title: Article 44 privatization policy issued
description: Supreme Leader Khamenei issues the 'General Policies of Article 44,' ordering transfer of up to 80% of shares in large state-owned enterprises (excluding core sectors such as upstream oil) to the private and cooperative sectors via public share offerings, reviving a stalled privatization program.
source_name: Iran Data Portal (Syracuse University) -- General Policies of Article 44

title: EU decides Iranian oil embargo
description: EU Council Decision 2012/35/CFSP bans imports of Iranian crude oil and petroleum products and freezes Central Bank of Iran assets, with a transition period allowing existing contracts to wind down through 1 July 2012.
source_name: EUR-Lex -- Council Decision 2012/35/CFSP

title: SWIFT disconnects sanctioned Iranian banks
description: Following the EU Council decision, SWIFT announces it will terminate financial-messaging services to roughly 30 EU-sanctioned Iranian banks effective 17 March 2012, cutting them off from the primary channel for international interbank payments.
source_name: SWIFT -- Press Release

title: Targeted Subsidies Reform phase two
description: The Rouhani government launches the subsidy program's second phase: petrol prices rise 75% (from 4,000 to 7,000 rials per litre) and cash-transfer eligibility is means-tested for the first time, requiring households to file income and asset declarations; the 2015-16 budget subsequently mandates further targeting of cash payments.
source_name: Economic Research Forum -- Iran's Subsidy Reform from Promise to Disappointment

title: US ends oil waivers -- push to zero exports
description: The Trump administration announces it will not renew sanctions waivers for the countries (China, India, Japan, South Korea, Turkey and others) still importing Iranian crude after 2 May 2019, aiming to cut Iran's oil exports to zero; exports fall from over 2.5 million bbl/day pre-sanctions to under 1 million bbl/day.
source_name: Al Jazeera

title: Raisi budget cuts National Development Fund savings rate
description: The Raisi government's first budget bill halves the share of oil revenue mandated for the National Development Fund's savings account -- from the 40% required by the fund's charter to 20% -- redirecting the freed funds to current government spending.
source_name: Iran International -- Iran Plans To Take More Money From National Reserves

title: Iran-Saudi Arabia relations restored
description: A Beijing-brokered agreement between Iran and Saudi Arabia re-establishes diplomatic relations and reopens embassies after a seven-year rupture, easing regional tensions with implications for regional trade and investment.
source_name: Al Jazeera

title: Iran formally joins BRICS
description: Iran's membership in the BRICS economic bloc (alongside Egypt, Ethiopia, and the UAE) takes effect 1 January 2024 after being invited in August 2023; Tehran frames membership as a hedge against Western economic isolation.
source_name: Tehran Times (state-media-attributed; accession date cross-checked against Atlantic Council and Stimson Center coverage)

title: Israel-Iran Twelve-Day War
description: Israel launches Operation Rising Lion, striking Iranian nuclear and military sites from 13 to 24 June 2025; Iran's oil exports fall to roughly 102,000 bbl/day mid-war, a refinery near Tehran and part of the South Pars gas field are damaged, and the rial depreciates sharply through the following weeks.
source_name: Al Jazeera

title: UN snapback sanctions reimposed
description: After the E3 (France, Germany, UK) trigger the JCPOA's snapback mechanism on 28 August 2025 citing Iranian non-compliance, the 30-day countdown lapses and UN Security Council sanctions under Resolutions 1696-1929 (arms embargo and proliferation-related financial and trade restrictions) are reinstated on 27-28 September 2025.
source_name: U.S. Department of State

title: Currency redenomination law passed
description: Parliament approves a law removing four zeros from the rial (10,000 old rials = 1 new rial) and introducing a subunit called the qeran; the Central Bank has two years to set implementation procedures, followed by a three-year dual-circulation transition, amid roughly 40% inflation and a rial that has lost over 90% of its value since 2018 sanctions reimposition.
source_name: Iran International

title: Rial replaces qran as national currency
description: Under the Unitary Currency Law of 1930-31, Bank Melli Iran assumes the monopoly on banknote issuance from the British-owned Imperial Bank of Persia (for a payment of £200,000), and the rial -- subdivided into 100 new dinars -- replaces the qran at par as Persia's official currency; Bank Melli's first banknotes (5, 10, 20, 100 and 500 rials) are issued starting April 1932.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica -- Eskenas (Banknotes)

title: OPEC founded with Iran as charter member
description: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Venezuela establish the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries at the Baghdad Conference (10-14 September 1960) to coordinate members' petroleum policies and resist unilateral posted-price cuts by Western oil majors; Iranian delegate Fuad Rouhani becomes OPEC's first Secretary-General.
source_name: United Nations Treaty Series -- OPEC founding resolutions (Vol. 443, No. 6363)

title: Third Development Plan launched
description: Iran's Plan Organization launches the Third Development Plan (September 1962-March 1968), the country's first comprehensive economic development plan, emphasizing heavy industry and supporting infrastructure while expanding private and public-sector banking to encourage investment in new industries.
source_name: Wikipedia -- Economic History of Iran (cross-checked against Encyclopaedia Iranica, Barnama-rizi)

title: Fourth Development Plan launched
description: The Fourth Development Plan (1968-73) shifts priority toward industry, agriculture and services -- especially transport and communications -- with reduced emphasis on the infrastructure-building focus of the Third Plan, aiming to raise GNP growth via industrial productivity and expand social-welfare services.
source_name: Wikipedia -- Economic History of Iran (cross-checked against Encyclopaedia Iranica, Barnama-rizi)

title: Fifth Development Plan Law ratified
description: The Majles ratifies the Fifth Development Plan Law, covering Farvardin 1352-Esfand 1356 (March 1973-March 1978) and restructuring the Plan Organization into the Plan and Budget Organization; within 18 months the plan is dramatically revised upward after the 1973-74 oil price shock nearly quadruples government oil revenue (see the 1974-08-01 entry on the Fifth Plan's revision).
source_name: Wikipedia -- Economic History of Iran (cross-checked against Encyclopaedia Iranica, Barnama-rizi)

title: Bonyad Mostazafan founded from confiscated Pahlavi assets
description: Ayatollah Khomeini decrees the creation of the Bonyad-e Mostazafan (Foundation of the Oppressed), successor to the Pahlavi Foundation, to administer assets confiscated from the former royal family and dozens of associates of the old regime; within a decade its holdings reportedly exceed $20bn across roughly 140 factories, 470 agribusinesses, 100 construction firms, 64 mines and 250 commercial companies, becoming one of several bonyad conglomerates operating outside normal budgetary and tax oversight.
source_name: Wikipedia -- Mostazafan Foundation (cross-checked against Wikipedia's Bonyad overview article)

title: Algiers Accords partially unfreeze Iranian assets
description: The Algiers Accords end the 444-day hostage crisis: the US agrees to unblock roughly $7.9-8bn of the approximately $12bn in Iranian assets frozen in November 1979, transfers 50 tonnes of gold held at the New York Federal Reserve, and creates the Iran-US Claims Tribunal at The Hague, with Iran depositing $1bn+ in escrow to settle outstanding claims -- a major but only partial unwinding of the 1979 asset freeze.
source_name: U.S. Department of State -- Iran-U.S. Claims Tribunal

title: US bans imports of Iranian-origin goods
description: Executive Order 12613 prohibits importing any goods or services of Iranian origin into the United States, citing Iranian state support for terrorism and attacks on US-flag and other neutral shipping in the Persian Gulf during the Iran-Iraq War; exceptions cover news materials, petroleum products refined from Iranian crude in third countries, and goods already exported from Iran before the order's effective date (12:01pm EST, 29 October 1987).
source_name: Ronald Reagan Presidential Library -- Executive Order 12613

title: US bans Iranian petroleum-development deals
description: Executive Order 12957 declares a national emergency with respect to Iran and prohibits US persons from financing, managing or supervising the development of Iranian petroleum resources, a narrower precursor to the comprehensive US trade and investment ban imposed two months later (Executive Order 12959, 6 May 1995).
source_name: The American Presidency Project (UC Santa Barbara) -- Executive Order 12957

title: UNSC Resolution 1696 demands enrichment suspension
description: The Security Council adopts Resolution 1696 (14-1, Qatar opposed), invoking Chapter VII Article 40 to demand Iran suspend all uranium-enrichment-related and reprocessing activities by 31 August 2006 or face the prospect of economic and diplomatic sanctions under Article 41; Iran does not comply, and the Council follows through with its first actual sanctions resolution (1737) that December.
source_name: United Nations -- Security Council Press Release SC/8792 (Resolution 1696)

title: UNSC Resolution 1747 expands arms embargo
description: Resolution 1747 bans Iran from exporting any arms or related materiel, calls on states and international financial institutions not to enter new grants, financial assistance or concessional loans with the Iranian government except for humanitarian or developmental purposes, and freezes the assets of 28 additional individuals and entities linked to Iran's nuclear and missile programs.
source_name: United Nations -- Security Council Press Release SC/8980 (Resolution 1747)

title: UNSC Resolution 1803 tightens bank and cargo vigilance
description: Resolution 1803 (14-0, Indonesia abstaining) calls on states to inspect, where reasonable grounds exist, the cargo of aircraft and vessels owned or operated by Iran Air Cargo and the Islamic Republic of Iran Shipping Line, and to exercise vigilance over transactions with all Iranian banks -- naming Bank Melli and Bank Saderat specifically -- to prevent financial flows linked to proliferation-sensitive nuclear activities.
source_name: United Nations -- Security Council Press Release SC/9268 (Resolution 1803)

title: CISADA expands US sanctions on fuel and financial sectors
description: President Obama signs the Comprehensive Iran Sanctions, Accountability, and Divestment Act (passed 408-8 House, 99-0 Senate), which targets firms supplying Iran with gasoline or refined petroleum products or supporting its domestic refining capacity -- aimed at Iran's reliance on imported gasoline despite being a crude exporter -- and mandates sanctions on foreign banks that knowingly facilitate significant transactions with the IRGC or its affiliates.
source_name: U.S. Congress -- Public Law 111-195 (CISADA)

title: NDAA Section 1245 targets Central Bank of Iran
description: Section 1245 of the FY2012 National Defense Authorization Act requires blocking the US-jurisdiction property of Iranian financial institutions including the Central Bank of Iran (CBI), and threatens foreign banks that knowingly conduct significant CBI transactions with loss of direct access to the US financial system -- a major escalation targeting Iran's oil-revenue payment channels that helps trigger the rial's collapse the following year.
source_name: U.S. Department of State -- Section 1245 of the NDAA for Fiscal Year 2012

title: Rial collapses amid sanctions squeeze
description: Under the combined weight of NDAA Central Bank sanctions, the EU oil embargo and the SWIFT disconnection, Iran's free-market rial loses roughly a quarter to 40% of its value against the US dollar within a single week, falling to about 35,000-40,000 rials/dollar versus roughly 10,000/dollar two years earlier; Tehran's Grand Bazaar merchants strike in protest on 3 October 2012.
source_name: NPR -- Currency In Crisis: Collapse Of Iran's Rial Continues

title: First Justice Shares distributed
description: Under the Article 44 privatization framework, the government begins distributing subsidized 'Justice Shares' (Saham-e Edalat) in state-owned enterprises to low-income households, war veterans' families and Basij members; by December 2006 roughly 4.6 million Iranians have received shares worth about $2.5bn (around $550/person), with a decade-long restriction on trading them.
source_name: Wikipedia -- Privatization in Iran

title: Telecommunication Company of Iran privatized to IRGC-linked consortium
description: A 50%-plus-one-share controlling stake in TCI is sold for $7.8bn to the Mobin Trust Consortium (Etemad-e Mobin) -- backed by the IRGC Cooperative Foundation, the Mostazafan Foundation and the Execution of Imam Khomeini's Order -- after the only private-sector competing bidder is disqualified on security grounds; the deal becomes the largest transaction in Tehran Stock Exchange history and a widely cited example of Article 44 'privatization' shifting state assets to parastatal/IRGC-linked entities rather than the private sector.
source_name: American Enterprise Institute (cross-checked against Wikipedia -- Telecommunication Company of Iran, citing AP/Reuters)

title: Khamenei issues Resistance Economy general policies
description: Supreme Leader Khamenei submits the 'General Policies of the Resistance Economy' (Eqtesad-e Moqavemati) to the Expediency Council under Article 110 of the constitution, directing the government to cut oil-revenue dependence, promote knowledge-based and domestic production, and build sanctions-resilience into economic planning; it becomes the dominant official framing for Iran's economic policy through the 2010s sanctions era.
source_name: Khamenei.ir (official Supreme Leader website; primary text of the policy directive, state-affiliated source used here only for the directive's own content, not a contested claim)

title: Rouhani unifies exchange rate, bans free-market trading
description: Facing rial depreciation ahead of the expected US JCPOA withdrawal, the government sets a single official rate of 42,000 rials/dollar and Vice President Jahangiri declares unauthorized currency trading equivalent to smuggling, subject to judiciary and security-force enforcement; the unified rate proves unsustainable and the black-market rate reaches roughly 70,000/dollar within a month.
source_name: Bourse & Bazaar Foundation

title: NIMA secondary forex market launched
description: The Central Bank launches the NIMA (Forex Management Integrated System) electronic platform, requiring exporters to sell foreign-currency earnings through registered exchange houses to importers of non-essential goods at a market-linked rate distinct from the official and open-market rates -- formalizing Iran's return to a multi-tier exchange-rate system just weeks after the failed April 2018 rate unification.
source_name: Atlantic Council -- Iran's Economy and the Burden of Multiple Exchange Rates

title: US designates IRGC a Foreign Terrorist Organization
description: The State Department designates the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) -- including its Quds Force and affiliated entities such as the Khatam al-Anbia construction/engineering conglomerate -- as a Foreign Terrorist Organization effective 15 April 2019, the first time the US has so designated part of another government's armed forces; the move raises legal exposure for any foreign firm found dealing with IRGC-linked companies, which control a large share of Iran's construction, telecom and energy-services sectors.
source_name: The White House (Trump Administration, archived) -- Statement on the IRGC FTO Designation

title: Justice Shares trading restriction lifted
description: Supreme Leader Khamenei approves a Rouhani-government request to let roughly 49 million Iranian holders of Justice Shares (first distributed from 2006) trade their shares directly on the Tehran Stock Exchange and Iran Fara Bourse, ending the decade-plus non-trading restriction and helping fuel a retail-investor stock market boom through mid-2020.
source_name: PressTV (state-media-attributed; cross-checked against Wikipedia -- Privatization in Iran for the 49-million-holder figure and market-boom context)

title: UNSC rejects US bid to extend Iran arms embargo
description: A US-sponsored resolution to indefinitely extend the UN conventional-arms embargo on Iran (due to expire under Resolution 2231's sunset clause) fails, with only the Dominican Republic joining the US in favor, Russia and China opposed, and the remaining 11 members abstaining; the vote sets up the Trump administration's contested unilateral 'snapback' claim weeks later.
source_name: Arms Control Association

title: Rial breaches 300,000/dollar on free market
description: Amid 'maximum pressure' sanctions, curtailed oil exports and pandemic-driven economic strain, Iran's open-market rial weakens past 300,000 rials/dollar for the first time -- roughly a 90% depreciation from pre-2018-sanctions levels -- deepening import-cost inflation.
source_name: Bloomberg

title: UN arms embargo on Iran expires
description: The 13-year UN conventional-arms embargo lapses on schedule under JCPOA/Resolution 2231's sunset clause despite the Trump administration's rejected unilateral 'snapback' claim of September 2020; the E3 (France, Germany, UK) and other Security Council members maintain the US -- having withdrawn from the JCPOA in 2018 -- has no standing to trigger snapback, and the embargo lapses as originally scheduled.
source_name: Al Jazeera

title: $6bn Iranian funds unfrozen via South Korea-Qatar transfer
description: As part of a prisoner-swap deal, the US issues a sanctions waiver letting roughly $6bn (5.573bn euros) in Iranian oil-revenue funds long frozen in South Korean banks move via Switzerland to restricted accounts at Qatar's Al-Ahli and Dukhan banks, usable only for humanitarian goods (food, medicine, agricultural/medical equipment) under Qatari and US oversight; five detained Americans are released in the accompanying swap on 18 September 2023.
source_name: The Washington Institute for Near East Policy

title: $6bn Qatar-held Iranian funds refrozen
description: Amid bipartisan pressure following Hamas's 7 October attack on Israel, the Biden administration reaches an agreement with Qatar to block any Iranian request to access the $6bn in oil-revenue funds transferred from South Korea the previous month, effectively refreezing money that had never actually been disbursed to Iran.
source_name: NPR

title: Great Persian Famine and 1918 influenza pandemic
description: Wartime allied grain requisitioning, blockade and disrupted trade routes during World War I trigger severe famine across Iran from 1917, compounded by the 1918-19 influenza pandemic, cholera and typhus under the weakened, occupied Qajar government; mainstream scholarship estimates roughly 2 million excess deaths (Mohammad Gholi Majd has argued for far higher tolls of 8-10 million, a figure not widely accepted among historians), devastating the rural agricultural labor force.
source_name: Wikipedia — Persian famine of 1917-1919 (cross-checked against Encyclopaedia Iranica's "Influenza" article and academic literature on death-toll estimates)

title: Azerbaijan Crisis reaches the UN Security Council
description: Amid Soviet-backed autonomous "People's Government" regimes established in Iranian Azerbaijan and Kurdistan (the Mahabad Republic) since December 1945, and Soviet troops remaining in northern Iran past the WWII withdrawal deadline, the UN Security Council adopts Resolution 2 on 30 January 1946 -- the first resolution in UN history -- addressing the dispute. Iran is pressured into a March 1946 deal granting the USSR 51% control of a joint north-Iran oil company before Soviet troops finally withdraw (6 May 1946); Iranian forces retake the region in December 1946, and the Majlis ultimately rejects the oil concession 102-2 in October 1947.
source_name: Wikipedia — Iran crisis of 1946 (cross-checked against Encyclopedia.com's "Azerbaijan Crisis" entry)

title: Buin Zahra earthquake
description: M7.2 earthquake devastates the Buin Zahra region west of Tehran, killing an estimated 12,225 people, injuring 2,776 and destroying over 21,000 houses; NOAA's contemporaneous damage estimate is $30 million, making it one of the deadliest disasters of the Pahlavi era and prompting Iran's first systematic seismic-hazard mapping efforts.
source_name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) — Significant Earthquake Database

title: Tabas earthquake
description: M7.4 earthquake devastates the city of Tabas and surrounding villages in South Khorasan province, killing an estimated 20,000 people and destroying roughly 15,000 houses; NOAA's contemporaneous damage estimate is $50 million. The disaster struck six weeks before the oil-workers' strikes that paralyzed the economy ahead of the 1979 revolution.
source_name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) — Significant Earthquake Database

title: Iran-Iraq War ends — ceasefire takes effect
description: Iran notifies the UN Secretary-General on 17-18 July 1988 that it accepts Security Council Resolution 598, after a deteriorating war economy and a string of Iraqi battlefield gains; the ceasefire takes effect at 3am on 20 August 1988 under UNIIMOG monitoring, ending eight years of war and opening the way for the Rafsanjani government's 1989 First Post-War Five-Year reconstruction plan.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Britannica

title: Manjil-Rudbar earthquake
description: M7.3 earthquake strikes Gilan and Zanjan provinces -- Iran's deadliest and costliest natural disaster of the modern era: an estimated 40,000 people killed, 105,000 injured, and roughly 100,000 houses destroyed. NOAA's Significant Earthquake Database estimates $7.2 billion in contemporaneous damage, striking less than two years after the Iran-Iraq War's ceasefire and compounding the reconstruction burden on Rafsanjani's First Post-War Five-Year Plan.
source_name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) — Significant Earthquake Database

title: Bam earthquake
description: M6.6 earthquake destroys much of the historic city of Bam in Kerman province, killing an estimated 31,000 people and leveling the UNESCO-listed Arg-e Bam citadel; NOAA's Significant Earthquake Database estimates $500 million in direct damage, while a subsequent World Bank post-disaster assessment put total reconstruction needs closer to $1.5 billion (see SOURCES.md Round 38 for the World Bank citation).
source_name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) — Significant Earthquake Database

title: Kermanshah earthquake
description: M7.3 earthquake on the Iran-Iraq border kills an estimated 630 people and destroys or damages over 15,500 houses in Kermanshah province, Iran's deadliest earthquake since Bam; NOAA's Significant Earthquake Database estimates $750 million in damage, while the Iranian government's own assessment put reconstruction costs closer to EUR 5 billion.
source_name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) — Significant Earthquake Database
